reference_translations.text: La pizza è stata inventata a Napoli.
reference_translations.notes: Standard compound passive; feminine-singular agreement on both participles.

reference_translations.text: La pizza venne inventata a Napoli.
reference_translations.notes: Passato remoto with venire, more narrative; also correct.

explanation: The passive of a past event uses essere in the past plus the participle: è stata inventata, with both participles feminine to agree with pizza. Avere never builds the passive.

common_errors.text: La pizza ha inventata a Napoli.
common_errors.note: WRONG: the passive is built on essere, not avere.

source_text: These houses were built in the fifteenth century.

reference_translations.text: Queste case sono state costruite nel Quattrocento.
reference_translations.notes: Feminine-plural agreement; Quattrocento is the idiomatic name for the 1400s.

reference_translations.text: Queste case furono costruite nel Quindicesimo secolo.
reference_translations.notes: Passato remoto variant; nel quindicesimo secolo is equally fine.

explanation: Case is feminine plural, so both participles take -e: sono state costruite. Either the passato prossimo (sono state costruite) or the passato remoto (furono costruite) fits a historical statement.

reference_translations.text: The guilty one will be punished.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: The culprit will be punished.
reference_translations.notes: culprit is a natural rendering of colpevole.

explanation: A future passive: sarà punito = will be punished. English mirrors it with 'will be' plus the participle.

source_text: The portrait was painted by Caravaggio.

reference_translations.text: Il ritratto è stato dipinto da Caravaggio.
reference_translations.notes: Agent with da; masculine-singular agreement.

reference_translations.text: Il ritratto fu dipinto da Caravaggio.
reference_translations.notes: Passato remoto, natural for a historical fact.

explanation: The doer named after a passive is the agent, introduced by da: da Caravaggio. Di would sound possessive ('of Caravaggio'). Ritratto is masculine singular, so dipinto stays -o.

common_errors.text: Il ritratto è stato dipinto di Caravaggio.
common_errors.note: WRONG: the agent takes da, not di (which reads as possessive).

source_text: The law was passed by the members of parliament.

reference_translations.text: La legge è stata approvata dai deputati.
reference_translations.notes: Agent da + i = dai; feminine-singular agreement on the participle.

reference_translations.text: La legge fu approvata dai deputati.
reference_translations.notes: Passato remoto variant.

explanation: The agent with a plural article contracts: da + i = dai deputati. Legge is feminine singular, so the participle is approvata.

source_text: The tickets are checked every morning.

reference_translations.text: I biglietti vengono controllati ogni mattina.
reference_translations.notes: venire for the recurring action; masculine-plural agreement.

reference_translations.text: I biglietti sono controllati ogni mattina.
reference_translations.notes: essere version, equally correct.

explanation: A repeated action reads well with venire: vengono controllati (get checked). Biglietti is masculine plural, so controllati. The essere version sono controllati is also fine.

source_text: La porta venne chiusa a chiave.

reference_translations.text: The door was locked.
reference_translations.notes: chiudere a chiave = to lock; English collapses it to one verb.

reference_translations.text: The door was locked shut.
reference_translations.notes: Acceptable, slightly heavier.

explanation: venne chiusa is the venire passive in the passato remoto (a simple tense, where venire is allowed). chiudere a chiave, literally 'close with a key', is English 'to lock'.

source_text: Le medicine vanno prese a stomaco pieno.

reference_translations.text: The medicines must be taken on a full stomach.
reference_translations.notes: andare + participle = obligation.

reference_translations.text: The medicines have to be taken on a full stomach.
reference_translations.notes: Equivalent.

explanation: vanno prese uses andare for obligation: must/have to be taken. Reading vanno as 'go' is the trap the negative reference guards against.

common_errors.text: The medicines go taken on a full stomach.
common_errors.note: WRONG: va/vanno here is obligation, not motion.

source_text: This form must be filled in in block capitals.

reference_translations.text: Questo modulo va compilato in stampatello.
reference_translations.notes: andare for obligation, the idiomatic choice for instructions.

reference_translations.text: Questo modulo deve essere compilato in stampatello.
reference_translations.notes: dovere + essere passive, also correct and slightly more explicit.

explanation: Instructions love andare for obligation: va compilato = must be filled in. The heavier deve essere compilato says the same thing and is accepted.

source_text: Bread is made here every day.

reference_translations.text: Qui il pane viene fatto ogni giorno.
reference_translations.notes: venire for the recurring process.

reference_translations.text: Qui il pane è fatto ogni giorno.
reference_translations.notes: essere version, also correct.

reference_translations.text: Qui si fa il pane ogni giorno.
reference_translations.notes: si passivante, the commonest everyday phrasing; valid alternative.

explanation: The recurring-process sense suits venire: viene fatto. Everyday Italian would often reach for the si construction instead (si fa il pane); both are offered.

source_text: Ogni domenica la messa veniva celebrata in latino.

reference_translations.text: Every Sunday mass was celebrated in Latin.
reference_translations.notes: Imperfect passive with venire for a habitual past action.

reference_translations.text: Every Sunday the mass would be celebrated in Latin.
reference_translations.notes: 'would be' captures the habitual imperfect.

explanation: veniva celebrata is the venire passive in the imperfect, for a repeated past action; English uses 'was celebrated' or the habitual 'would be celebrated'.

reference_translations.text: Il presidente è stato eletto ieri.
reference_translations.notes: Passive, the news register this English implies.

reference_translations.text: Hanno eletto il presidente ieri.
reference_translations.notes: Active with a vague 'they'; what speech would use.

explanation: English passives map onto either an Italian passive or an active with an unspecified 'they'; the register decides. In a bulletin the passive is right; at the bar the active is.

source_text: Il bilancio è presentato ogni anno ai finanziatori.

reference_translations.text: The balance sheet is presented to the investors every year.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: The accounts are presented to the backers every year.
reference_translations.notes: Also fine.

explanation: A formal Italian passive lands as a formal English passive: the register matches on both sides. Note this is a statement, not an obligation; 'va presentato' would be the obligation version.

reference_translations.text: Qui si vendono libri usati.
reference_translations.notes: The si passivante, idiomatic for signage; agrees with libri.

reference_translations.text: Qui i libri usati sono venduti.
reference_translations.notes: Grammatical full passive, but stiff for a sign.

explanation: With no doer to name, Italian prefers si. The verb agrees with the thing: libri is plural, so si vendono.

common_errors.note: WRONG as standard: the verb agrees with the plural noun, si vendono.

source_text: The books are sold by the Feltrinelli bookshop.

reference_translations.text: I libri sono venduti dalla libreria Feltrinelli.
reference_translations.notes: Named agent, so the full passive is the only option.

explanation: Naming the seller rules the si construction out: si exists to leave the doer unnamed. The full passive takes the agent after da, contracted with the article: da + la = dalla.

common_errors.text: Si vendono libri dalla libreria Feltrinelli.
common_errors.note: WRONG: si can never take a da-agent.

reference_translations.text: Rooms to let here.
reference_translations.notes: Sign register.

reference_translations.text: Rooms are rented out here.
reference_translations.notes: Fuller, also correct.

explanation: The si passivante has no English twin, so it lands as a passive or as sign shorthand. The plural agreement (affittano) follows camere.

reference_translations.text: Two men were arrested.
reference_translations.notes: English prefers the passive where Italian used a vague-they active.

reference_translations.text: They arrested two men.
reference_translations.notes: Literal; fine if the 'they' is identifiable from context.

explanation: The asymmetry runs both ways: Italian's vague-they active is most naturally English's passive. Translating it as 'they arrested' invents a 'they' the Italian never committed to.

reference_translations.text: Il volo delle 18 è stato cancellato.
reference_translations.notes: Passive, the register of an official notice.

reference_translations.text: Hanno cancellato il volo delle 18.
reference_translations.notes: Active; what a passenger would say to another passenger.

explanation: An airport notice keeps the doer invisible, so the passive fits; between passengers the active is more natural. Same content, different register.

source_text: Bread is made here every day.

reference_translations.text: Qui si fa il pane ogni giorno.
reference_translations.notes: si construction, what speech uses.

reference_translations.text: Qui il pane viene fatto ogni giorno.
reference_translations.notes: Dynamic passive with venire; correct, slightly more formal.

explanation: For what generally happens, everyday Italian reaches for si rather than the full passive. Both are offered because the register line here is soft, not sharp.
